subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed some college coursework, but did not earn a degree.
Attended courses relevant to broadcasting, media studies, and communications.
Has participated in informal on-the-job training specific to radio operations and playlist curation.
No formal broadcasting certification, but learned core skills through practical experience.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Broadcast Announcer and Radio Disc Jockey.
Responsible for curating and sequencing music playlists for radio shows.
Hosts live radio shows, making announcements, presenting music, and reading Public Service Announcements (PSAs).
Involved in managing show timing and real-time playlist adjustments.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the broadcasting and media industry.
Focuses specifically on radio broadcasting and music entertainment.
Operates within the broader entertainment and communications sector.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works for a small to mid-sized radio station typical of local or community radio.
Environment is informal and collaborative, with a hands-on approach to operations.
Team likely consists of a mix of volunteers and paid staff.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Operates in the non-commercial/community radio segment.
Serves a diverse local audience with a mix of music genres and PSAs.
Works in a segment that values eclectic playlists and local community engagement.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a broadcast announcer/radio DJ.
Role tenure aligns with entry-level or early-career professional.
Began hosting and playlist curation responsibilities within the last two years.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Functions at a junior or early-career professional level.
Primary responsibilities are operational, with some creative autonomy.
Not in a management or supervisory role.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Curates and sequences music playlists for radio shows.
Hosts live broadcasts, introducing songs and delivering PSAs as scheduled.
Monitors song timing to align with station guidelines and PSA requirements.
Edits playlists in real time in response to changes or technical issues.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends the majority of work hours (around 60-70%) on playlist curation and sequencing.
Live hosting and on-air broadcasting take up about 20-30% of time.
Administrative tasks such as archiving playlists and logging PSAs occupy the remaining time.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has significant autonomy in selecting and ordering songs, within FCC and station guidelines.
Independently manages real-time playlist adjustments during live shows.
Adheres to pre-set PSA schedules and content rules, but has creative freedom in show curation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Selecting songs within preferred genres and familiar artists is intuitive.
Announcing songs and engaging with the audience on-air comes naturally.
Managing the physical setup (vinyl, CDs) is routine and comfortable due to hands-on experience.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Ensuring all song selections comply with FCC content and timing regulations can be complex.
Sequencing music to achieve a coherent flow across multiple genres requires careful planning.
Adjusting live playlists in response to technical issues or unexpected timing changes is challenging.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Archiving playlists and logging setlists into Spinatron is repetitive.
Scheduling and logging PSAs at precise intervals involves routine data entry.
Checking song metadata for compliance and duration is repetitive and could be automated.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Curating creative playlists and discovering new song combinations is highly engaging.
Live interaction with listeners and hosting the show provides satisfaction.
Manual data entry for PSA logs and archiving playlists tends to feel tedious.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Switching between physical formats and digital platforms slows down transitions.
Manual playlist archiving and PSA logging are time-consuming.
Last-minute changes to show timing require rapid, sometimes stressful, adjustments.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Enjoyment is highest when curating and sequencing creative playlists, which also leverages core skills.
Productivity drops during repetitive administrative work, which is less enjoyable.
Skill development is fastest in tasks that are both challenging and enjoyable, such as live show hosting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to trying new technologies, especially if they improve workflow.
Sees practical value in adopting tools that streamline playlist management or show scheduling.
Prefers hands-on experimentation over formal training.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts reasonably well to new software, though initial learning can be challenging.
Comfortable with mainstream platforms like Spotify and Google Docs after brief acclimation.
Requires more time to adapt to specialized radio or AI tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to invest time in learning tools that have clear benefits for daily work.
Less inclined to pursue in-depth training unless required for the role.
Prefers learning through guided tutorials or peer mentorship over self-study manuals.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Main motivation is increasing efficiency and reducing repetitive tasks.
Barriers include time constraints and unclear relevance to immediate responsibilities.
Adoption is easier when recommended or demonstrated by trusted colleagues.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peer recommendations strongly influence willingness to try new technology.
Management encouragement increases adoption, especially when accompanied by training.
Observing successful use by others boosts confidence in a new tool's value.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Spinatron for playlist storage, archiving, and logging.
Regularly utilizes Google Docs and Microsoft Word for show notes and scripts.
Streams and checks music on Spotify for digital playlist curation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on physical music formats such as vinyl records and CDs for live shows.
Uses standard radio broadcasting equipment for hosting and announcements.
Employs a desktop or laptop computer for digital file management and documentation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with radio automation basics, though not using advanced automation tools daily.
Comfortable with mainstream music and document management platforms.
Limited experience with more complex broadcast scheduling or analytics software.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Transitioning between physical and digital music formats can be cumbersome.
Playlist updates on Spinatron must be manually synced with live show adjustments.
Occasional compatibility issues when converting between file formats for different platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Has some experience using Large Language Models (LLMs) for work tasks.
Understands basic AI concepts such as text generation and content suggestion.
Familiar with common AI terms like 'prompt', 'model', and 'training data', but not deeply technical.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has used ChatGPT or similar LLM-based tools for brainstorming or text drafting.
No regular use of specialized broadcast AI tools (e.g., music recommendation engines) yet.
Occasionally leverages general-purpose AI tools integrated into document editors.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools occasionally, primarily for generating show notes or brainstorming playlist ideas.
AI may be consulted for finding thematically related songs or creating engaging announcements.
Not integrated as a core part of daily workflow yet; use is ad hoc and task-specific.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated song pairings have sometimes inspired creative playlist ideas.
Experience with LLMs has occasionally produced generic or irrelevant suggestions.
Learned to provide more specific prompts to improve AI usefulness for show content.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training provided by the organization to date.
Peers occasionally share tips or tutorials on using LLMs for broadcast scripting.
Self-guided experimentation and online resources are main sources for AI learning.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to use AI tools independently for ideation, then reviews outputs before integrating.
Occasionally uses AI in a step-by-step process for brainstorming or writing scripts.
Values manual review of AI-generated content to ensure fit with show style and compliance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for idea generation and content drafting.
Relies on human judgment for final curation and sequencing decisions.
Treats AI suggestions as starting points, not definitive answers.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to exploring more AI-driven workflows if they save time or increase creativity.
Cautious about fully automating playlist curation due to need for personal touch.
Would consider adopting AI-driven tools for administrative tasks like logging.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and content generation.
Finds natural language prompts more intuitive than structured commands.
Would use command-based interfaces for batch administrative tasks if available.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Playlist curation relies heavily on personal taste and understanding of audience preferences.
Knowledge of FCC regulations and local station policies is essential for content compliance.
Live show hosting requires improvisational and interpersonal skills that are hard to codify.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI for brainstorming and admin support, but not for final playlist decisions.
Always reviews AI-generated suggestions for compliance and fit with station ethos.
Reluctant to rely on AI for tasks where errors could breach regulations.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI support for repetitive or data-heavy tasks like archiving and PSA logging.
Believes creative and compliance-sensitive decisions should remain human-led.
Sees value in AI-human collaboration for ideation, not execution.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for back-office admin work, provided manual override is available.
Would feel threatened by automation of core creative and hosting responsibilities.
Open to automation if it demonstrably enhances, rather than replaces, personal input.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI can enhance creative output by suggesting new ideas.
Concerns that over-reliance on AI could erode unique personal style.
Sees FCC compliance knowledge and live show skills as AI-resistant.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning prompt engineering and effective AI usage is becoming relevant.
Monitoring and validating AI outputs adds a layer of responsibility.
Adapting workflows to integrate AI suggestions is an emerging skill.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about automation of playlist archiving and basic logging.
Does not believe core live hosting or creative curation will be replaced soon.
Watches industry trends to stay aware of evolving automation risks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews AI-generated suggestions before use.
No fully automated processes in current workflow; human approval is required.
Would insist on override mechanisms if AI-driven playlisting or scheduling is introduced.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: No formal AI adoption policy at the station yet.
Peers are experimenting with AI informally, sharing results as a community.
Management is open to AI experimentation but has not mandated adoption.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a tool that can streamline media workflows and inspire creativity.
Recognizes risks of job displacement in highly automatable roles.
Believes AI will create new opportunities for content personalization and efficiency.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes human oversight is essential to prevent inappropriate or biased outputs.
Concerned about potential loss of individuality in creative industries.
Supports transparent disclosure when AI-generated content is used.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from an AI tool that suggests song sequences based on lyrical and stylistic compatibility.
Desires automation in administrative tasks like PSA scheduling and playlist archiving.
Wishes for AI-enhanced compliance checks for FCC and station rules.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects AI will handle more of the administrative and compliance workload.
Believes creative and hosting aspects will remain human-driven but supported by AI tools.
Anticipates needing to regularly update skills to work alongside evolving AI technologies.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Recommends offering workshops or peer-led training on AI tool usage.
Advocates for clear guidelines on where and how AI can be used in broadcasting.
Supports implementing pilot programs to test AI in non-critical tasks before wider rollout.
